title: Compute Row Relative Frequency
description: Calculate the relative frequency within a row of a two-way table by dividing a cell count by the row total and converting to a percentage.
condition: Given a two-way table with a specific row category, a cell count in that row, and the row total.
response: Divide the cell count by the row total and express the result as a percentage.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Applies to any row-based relative frequency calculation.

title: Create segmented bar graph from relative frequencies
description: Represent row relative frequencies as proportional segments within equal-length bars.
condition: Given relative frequency percentages for categories within groups.
response: Draw one bar per group with segments sized according to percentages summing to 100%.
label: Production / Skill
example: For adults: draw a bar to 100% with 31% labeled pizza and 69% labeled hot dog.

title: Draw positive non-linear association
description: Create a scatter plot showing a generally increasing trend that is not linear.
condition: Asked to represent a positive association that is not linear.
response: Plot points that increase overall but follow a curved or non-straight pattern.
label: Production / Skill
example: Plot points that rise as $x$ increases but curve upward rather than forming a straight line.

title: Find missing values in two-way table
description: Use row and column totals to determine missing frequencies in a two-way table.
condition: Given a partially completed two-way table with some row or column totals provided.
response: Use subtraction within rows or columns and addition across totals to fill in missing entries.
label: Principle
example: If total for 7th grade is 293 and 219 prefer bike, then prefer walk $= 293 - 219 = 74$.

title: Identify Clusters in a Scatter Plot
description: Recognize groups of data points concentrated within a specific range of x-values (or region of the graph).
condition: Given a scatter plot with visible groupings of points in certain x-value intervals.
response: State the approximate x-value ranges where clusters of points appear.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If many points occur for $5 \le x \le 20$ and another group for $30 \le x \le 45$, identify these intervals as clusters.

title: Identify negative association
description: Recognize when two variables have a negative association based on contextual description.
condition: Given a pair of variables described in context.
response: Determine whether as one variable increases, the other tends to decrease.
label: Concept
example: As number of absences increases, final grades tend to decrease → negative association.

title: Identify Non-Linear Association
description: Determine that a scatter plot shows a non-linear association when points do not follow a steady increasing or decreasing linear trend.
condition: Given a scatter plot where points rise and fall or curve rather than align around a straight line.
response: State that the association is non-linear and justify based on the changing pattern.
label: Concept
example: If as $x$ increases, $y$ sometimes increases and sometimes decreases, the association is non-linear.
notes: Concept of non-linear trend based on visual pattern.

title: Identify outlier in scatter plot
description: Recognize and mark a data point that does not follow the general pattern of a scatter plot.
condition: Given a scatter plot with one point far from the overall trend.
response: Circle or identify the point that lies far from the general trend.
label: Production / Skill
example: In a downward trend, circle a single point far above the fitted line.

title: Identify Positive Association
description: Recognize that a scatter plot shows positive association when y-values tend to increase as x-values increase.
condition: Given a scatter plot where points trend upward from left to right.
response: State that the association is positive and describe that y increases as x increases.
label: Concept
example: If larger diameters correspond to greater heights, the scatter plot shows a positive association.

title: Interpret Association from Relative Frequencies
description: Determine whether two categorical variables are associated by comparing relative frequencies across categories.
condition: Given relative frequencies for different categories of one variable across levels of another variable.
response: Conclude there is an association if the relative frequencies differ substantially across categories; otherwise conclude there is likely no association.
label: Principle / Model
example: Fire creatures: 44% have power < 100, while water creatures: 88% have power < 100. Because these percentages differ greatly, there is an association between element and power.
notes: Requires contextual explanation referencing compared percentages.

title: Interpret Axis Labels and Units
description: Determine what each axis represents and interpret values according to the stated units (e.g., thousands of dollars).
condition: Given a graph with labeled axes that include variable names and units.
response: Identify the quantity and unit represented on each axis and scale values accordingly.
label: Rule / Plan
example: The x-axis shows advertising in thousands of dollars, so 26 represents $26{,}000.
notes: Supports correct interpretation of plotted values.

title: Interpret Scatter Plot Point in Context
description: Explain the meaning of a specific plotted point using the real-world context of the variables and their units.
condition: Given a labeled scatter plot and a specific ordered pair (x, y).
response: State what the x-value and y-value represent in context, including units.
label: Rule / Plan
example: The point $(26, 76.5)$ means the business spent $26{,}000 on advertising and earned $76{,}500 in income that month.
notes: Requires mapping coordinate values to axis labels and units.

title: Interpret Slope in Context
description: Explain the meaning of the slope of a linear model in terms of the context of the variables.
condition: Given a linear model such as $y = mx + b$ representing a real-world relationship.
response: State that for each 1-unit increase in x, y changes by m units, interpreted in context.
label: Principle / Model
example: For $y = \frac{7}{10}x + 3.8$, the slope $\frac{7}{10}$ means the height increases $\frac{7}{10}$ inch for each additional inch of diameter.
notes: Based on definition of slope as rate of change.

title: Plot Ordered Pair on Scatter Plot
description: Given numerical values for two variables, place the corresponding ordered pair correctly on a scatter plot with appropriate scales.
condition: Given an ordered pair (x, y) and a scatter plot with labeled axes and scale.
response: Plot a point at the horizontal value x and vertical value y on the graph.
label: Production / Skill
example: To add November's data, plot the point $(25, 95)$ on the graph.
notes: Requires coordinating axis scales and ordered pair structure.

title: Recognize no association from equal proportions
description: Determine that two categorical variables show no association when corresponding conditional proportions are equal.
condition: Given a two-way table where conditional proportions for one variable are the same across categories of the other variable.
response: Conclude there is no association and use proportional reasoning to find missing counts if needed.
label: Principle
example: If $\frac{112}{112+52} = \frac{x}{x+84}$, solve for $x$ to maintain equal proportions (giving $x=181$).

title: Row Percentages Sum to 100%
description: Recognize that relative frequencies within a row must sum to 100%.
condition: Given all relative frequencies for categories within a single row of a two-way table.
response: Ensure or state that the percentages add to 100%.
label: Concept
notes: Supports checking correctness of computed relative frequencies.

title: Select Better Fitting Line by Visual Fit
description: Choose the linear model that best fits a scatter plot by judging which line passes through the middle of the data and follows the overall trend.
condition: Given a scatter plot with two or more proposed linear models drawn.
response: Select the line that more closely follows the overall pattern and has points balanced above and below it.
label: Production / Skill
example: Between $y=\frac{4}{5}x+3.5$ and $y=\frac{7}{10}x+3.8$, choose the line that runs through the center of the data cloud.
notes: Primarily perceptual judgment of fit rather than formal calculation.

title: Sketch reasonable line of best fit
description: Draw a line that represents the general linear trend in a scatter plot.
condition: Given a scatter plot showing an approximately linear trend.
response: Sketch a straight line that follows the overall direction and center of the data points.
label: Production / Skill
example: Draw a downward-sloping line through the middle of points showing soup sales decreasing as temperature increases.

title: Use linear equation to make prediction
description: Substitute a given input value into a linear equation to compute a predicted output.
condition: Given a linear equation in two variables and a specific input value for the independent variable.
response: Substitute the value into the equation and evaluate to find the predicted output.
label: Principle
